name: Los Angeles County, California
tagline: The nation's largest local government — ~10M residents, ~$50.3B budget, 38 departments. Enterprise IT direction sits with the Chief Information Office (CIO) in the Chief Executive Office; buying flows through the Internal Services Department (ISD) and Board of Supervisors approval.
overview: Los Angeles County is the most populous county in the United States — roughly 10 million residents, larger than most U.S. states — governed by a five-member Board of Supervisors and run day-to-day through a County Chief Executive Office (CEO) overseeing roughly three dozen departments. Enterprise technology direction lives with the County Chief Information Office (CIO), which sits inside the CEO and oversees technology, data, security, and privacy countywide; the Chief Data Office, Privacy Office, and Chief Information Security Office report up through it. Operational IT, telecom, and — critically for sellers — most procurement run through the Internal Services Department (ISD). The County is best understood as a federated buyer: the CIO sets strategy and governance, large departments (Health Services, DHS, Sheriff, DPSS, the courts-adjacent agencies) carry their own substantial IT budgets and run their own modernization, and ISD plus the Board of Supervisors gate the contracts. For a Dell SLG seller, this is one of the largest single local-government opportunities in the country, but the entry point is the procurement machinery and the department-level buying centers, not a single central IT wallet.

budget.intro: The Board unanimously adopted a $50.3B FY2026-27 budget in late June 2026, up ~$1.5B from the prior year (the April 2026 Recommended Budget was $47.9B; the increase came largely from shifting ~$1.3B in restricted/special funds and ~$900M in new state behavioral-health money). It remains a cost-pressure cycle: the County carries a combined ~$4.8B AB 218 childhood-sexual-assault settlement liability (with ~150 new claims/month), plus federal/state funding risk. The adopted plan holds services largely intact but is roughly flat on headcount (a net ~202-position reduction to ~115,683 budgeted positions). IT spend is distributed across departments rather than concentrated in one central line.

stats.label: FY2026-27 adopted budget
stats.note: Adopted June 2026; up ~$1.5B from prior year (Recommended was $47.9B in April 2026)

stats.label: AB 218 settlement liability
stats.note: 11,000+ childhood-sexual-assault cases, ~150 new claims/month — the dominant fiscal pressure

stats.label: County employees
stats.note: Scale of the ERP / payroll / HR systems the CIO oversees

notes: IT spending is federated: large departments (Health Services, Sheriff, Public Social Services) hold their own IT budgets and run their own modernization, so there is no single county-wide 'IT budget' line to target.

FY2026-27 is a cost-pressure year — the ~$4.8B AB 218 settlement liability, litigation exposure, and federal/state funding risk squeeze discretionary spend, which favors efficiency, automation, and consolidation pitches over net-new capital.

Always verify a department's own appropriation before forecasting; the County is mid-transition under Measure G (2024 voter-approved) toward an elected county executive (2028) and a nine-member board (by 2032).

initiatives.name: Generative AI adoption & AI Implementation Plan
initiatives.summary: The County is operationalizing generative AI countywide — an AI Implementation Plan with a 2026-27 phase to stand up AI Transformation Teams and bake responsible-AI practices into procurement and legal review, plus an annual County AI Professional Development Summit. AI-infrastructure and data-platform conversations are live.

initiatives.name: Cloud data platforms & RPA Center of Excellence
initiatives.summary: The CIO has championed modern cloud data platforms and a Center of Excellence for Robotic Process Automation to drive automation and information sharing across departments — a fit for infrastructure, data, and as-a-service plays.

initiatives.name: Regional cyber resilience ahead of 2026/2028 mega-events
initiatives.summary: LA is hardening critical-infrastructure cyber defenses ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup and 2028 Olympic & Paralympic Games. A Regional Cyber Coalition and a multi-county Threat Intelligence Sharing Platform are sharing real-time threat intel across the greater LA region.

initiatives.name: Enterprise ERP & shared systems
initiatives.summary: The CIO oversees countywide Financials, Budget, Payroll, Personnel, HR, and property-tax systems serving ~110K employees — a long-running modernization and infrastructure-refresh track underpinning the whole enterprise.

procurement.intro: Selling to LA County means navigating its procurement machinery, not a single buying desk. ISD's Procurement division buys goods and services for County departments and provides governance through the Office of Countywide Contracting; the Board of Supervisors must approve service contracts over $100,000. The County also participates in cooperative purchasing to extend other jurisdictions' pricing and terms. Confirm which department owns the requirement, then map the ISD path and any co-op vehicle.

vehicles.name: ISD Procurement / Office of Countywide Contracting
vehicles.detail: The central purchasing function for County departments; runs solicitations posted on the LA County Solicitations (LACoBids) portal and administers master agreements departments buy against.

vehicles.name: Board of Supervisors approval (>$100K)
vehicles.detail: Service contracts above $100,000 require Board approval — factor Board agenda timing and supplier-requirement compliance into any sizable deal.

vehicles.name: Cooperative purchasing consortiums
vehicles.detail: The County participates in purchasing consortiums/cooperatives to extend pre-negotiated pricing and terms — a faster path than a stand-alone County solicitation where a usable co-op contract exists.

vehicles.name: Department-delegated purchasing
vehicles.detail: Departments have delegated authority for smaller buys; ISD assists with purchases beyond that delegation. Identify whether a requirement clears at the department level or must route through ISD.

powerStructure.name: Peter Loo
powerStructure.title: County Chief Information Officer (CIO)
powerStructure.note: Made permanent CIO June 26, 2024 after years as acting CIO; 30+ years in County IT. Owns enterprise technology, data, security, and privacy strategy — the top enterprise-IT relationship.

powerStructure.name: James Thurmond
powerStructure.title: Acting Chief Information Security Officer (CISO)
powerStructure.note: Leads countywide information-security strategy and governance, directing Departmental Information Security Officers (DISOs). The security buying-center owner; confirm current/permanent status before forecasting.

powerStructure.name: Michael Owh
powerStructure.title: Director, Internal Services Department (ISD)
powerStructure.note: Runs the department that owns countywide procurement; former chief procurement officer for the City of LA and New York City. The procurement gatekeeper for most County buys.

powerStructure.name: Joseph M. Nicchitta
powerStructure.title: Chief Executive Officer (County CEO)
powerStructure.note: Made permanent CEO by the Board on May 19, 2026 after serving as acting/interim CEO since October 2025; previously chief deputy CEO / COO since 2020. Succeeds Fesia Davenport, who resigned effective April 16, 2026 (health; amid a contested $2M settlement tied to Measure G). Heads the Chief Executive Office, which the CIO sits within and which builds the County budget — the executive context for any enterprise-scale program.

powerStructure.name: Hilda L. Solis
powerStructure.title: Chair, Board of Supervisors (1st District, 2026)
powerStructure.note: The Board is the County's governing body and approves contracts >$100K. Board chairship rotates annually; District 1 and 3 seats are up for election in November 2026.

news.title: Board unanimously adopts $50.3B FY2026-27 budget (up ~$1.5B), services largely intact
news.impact: Bigger topline but still cost-pressured by a ~$4.8B AB 218 settlement liability and federal/state funding risk — lead with efficiency, automation, and consolidation, not net-new capital.

news.title: Joseph M. Nicchitta named permanent County CEO; succeeds Fesia Davenport
news.impact: New executive at the top of the CEO office (where the CIO sits). Nicchitta ran the office as interim CEO from Oct 2025; Davenport resigned April 16, 2026. Re-anchor executive relationships and budget narrative to the new CEO.

news.title: Board adopts FY2025-26 Supplemental Budget with ~5.5% cuts, ~1,125 positions eliminated
news.impact: Confirms an austerity posture — lead with efficiency, automation, and consolidation; expect tighter scrutiny on new capital.

news.title: LA County AI Professional Development Summit convenes business and IT leaders
news.impact: AI is an active, executive-sponsored priority — an opening for AI-infrastructure and data-platform conversations.

news.title: Regional Cyber Coalition / multi-county Threat Intelligence Sharing Platform advances
news.impact: Cyber resilience ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup and 2028 Olympics is a funded, regional priority — a security and infrastructure entry point.

news.title: Peter Loo confirmed as permanent County CIO
news.impact: Stable, long-tenured leadership at the top of enterprise IT — a durable relationship to build.

dellActions: Map the federated buying centers — CIO plus new CEO Joseph Nicchitta for enterprise strategy, but Health Services, Sheriff, and DPSS hold large independent IT budgets. Pursue department-level programs, not a single central IT line.
Lead with efficiency and consolidation in an austerity year: position infrastructure refresh, automation (RPA), and AI-ready data platforms as cost-takeout, not net-new capital.
Tie ISG / AI-infrastructure to the County's generative-AI and cloud-data-platform push and the AI Implementation Plan's 2026-27 operational phase.
Engage the security buying center (CISO James Thurmond, DISOs) on cyber resilience ahead of the 2026 World Cup and 2028 Olympics, including the Regional Cyber Coalition.
Get the procurement path right early: confirm ISD/Office of Countywide Contracting routing, usable cooperative-purchasing vehicles, and Board-approval timing for any contract over $100K.
